title: Ben “Emilio” Ojemann
text:
Hi! My name is Ben Ojemann, though I also go by Emilio. I am a third-year undergraduate, double majoring in Hydrologic Sciences & Policy (B.S.) and Physical Geography (B.S.) at the University of California, Santa Barbara.
I grew up in Seattle, WA where the Pacific Northwest instilled in me a deep love for the great outdoors and the environment. After moving away for college at UCSB, I have committed myself to improving natural resources through rigorous coursework, field work, and research.

title: The Outdoors
text:
I spend lots of my time thinking about the environment in an academic context. But, my most formative experiences with nature have less to do with lab deadlines or a GIS software, and more reminding myself of its real-life beauty. Here are some outdoor trips that have stuck with me, and I would highly recommend!


Canoeing the Colorado River

October 2023 | UCSB Adventure Programs

This one was equal parts adventure and personal development. I went as a part of the Leadership Training Course, an outdoor program offered by UCSB Recreation to students looking to become certified trip leaders. The group and I took a multi-day canoe trip down the part of Colorado River splitting Nevada and Arizona states. We visited the Hoover Dam, developed proper paddling technique, and soaked in hot springs along the way. The trip ended with an early 6am push to get back to Santa Barbara in time for class. With our headlamps strapped on, we found ourselves canoeing in nearly-pure darkness. That was easily one of the more surreal mornings of my life.




Hiking Blackcomb Mountain, Whistler

September 2024 | Family Trip
  
We started the morning with a gondola ride up to the mountain’s trailhead, giving us time to grab brunch before the day hike. It was long, tiring, and at one point very questionably navigated… we were briefly lost. But, the views made the entire trip completely worth it, and the photos here don’t do it justice. Surprisingly, Whistler in the middle of summer is still rather busy at the base, but your surroundings quickly get quiet once you are up high enough. There ended up being long stretches of the trail that we essentially had to ourselves. I gave the day a solid 5 out of 5 stars a year and a half ago, and would eagerly go back up tomorrow.




Backpacking in Olympic National Park

July 2025 | Brothers Weekend
My two brothers and I backpacked into the Olympic backcountry, stopping at two sites before camping at Royal Lake. I can attest that this spot is indeed a “hidden gem.” Though we lost the water filter somewhere along our trek up - so every drop of water ended up boiled over the stove - we survived. The nights there were the quietest I’d experienced in a long while. We played the guitar by a fire after every dinner, swam in the frigid alpine lake, and soaked in the blissful distance from our laptops.

title: Resume
text: Download my Resume (PDF)

title: About Me
text:
Read about who I am and what keeps me going outside of the lab.

Where I’m From
I was born in the beautiful city of Mazatenango, Guatemala before permanently relocating to Seattle, Washington.
 
Growing up in Seattle largely shaped who I am today. I was lucky being surrounded by the towering Cascade Range, lush evergreen forests, strong rivers, and dramatic coastlines. I have always been drawn to the outdoors, and this connection is a big reason for why I ended up studying the environment.



Photograph: Johnny Walker





What I’m doing at UCSB
I am currently working towards earning two degrees at UC Santa Barbara: one in Hydrology and the other in Physical Geography. The trajectory of my academic and professional path is guided by an obsession with water and my experiences exploring the diverse California landscapes. On the hydrology side, I’ve worked through coursework covering ground and surface water systems, soil science, and policy governing the management of these resources. On the geography side, I’ve completed two full GIS series where I built up technical skills in spatial analysis, cartography, and remote sensing in ArcGIS, Google Earth Engine, and Python.
Beyond the coursework, I am a Student Research Lead at the university’s Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration, where I conduct ecological research, work with interns, and contribute to wetland restoration at the North Campus Open Space (NCOS).
 



Hobbies
When I’m not in the field or staring at satellite imagery, you’ll probably find me on a nearby pitch, playing ultimate frisbee. I’ve played competitively since middle school, and now lead a UCSB club team to victory as a captain. It is among my favorite ways to relieve stress, connect with people, and occasionally hit the grass for a layout!



Photograph: David Palmer

title: About Me
text:
I was born in the beautiful city of Mazatenango, Guatemala before relocating to Seattle, Washington.
 Growing up in Seattle largely shaped who I am today. I was lucky to have been surrounded by the towering Cascades mountains, powerful rivers, lush forests, and strong coastlines. I was instantly drawn the outdoors. This connection is a big reason why I ended up studying the environment.

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
Overview
The North Campus Open Space (NCOS) is an actively restoring estuary situated directly adjacent to the UC Santa Barbara campus. As a Student Research Lead at the Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration, I designed and led my own study investigating the spatial and trophic relationships between local waterbirds and aquatic invertebrates across four habitat zones in the upper arms of the Devereux Slough.



Photograph: Bill Dewey





The Question
As NCOS continues to restore its historic tidal connectivity and diverse, native habitats, a key ecological management question emerges: how are waterbirds and their invertebrate prey distributed across the wetland, and how do habitat zones influence those patterns? Getting a good understanding of these these linkages can directly inform restoration decisions such as deciding where to prioritize habitat work.



Methods
To investigate these patterns, I combined monthly waterbird survey data with quarterly aquatic invertebrate sampling across four distinct habitat zones at the Devereux Slough: the main channel and its mudflats, the Phelps Creek confluence, constructed freshwater ponds, and seasonal vernal pools. Each zone was ecologically distinct (different salinities, water depths, and vegetation structures) despite being a part of the same restoration site. After linking the invertebrate community in each zone with published waterbird diets, I built trophic networks: visual maps of who is eating what, and where. All spatial analysis and network visualizations were carried out in ArcGIS and R.



Results
The main channel and mudflats support the greatest waterbird diversity at NCOS, with a dense, generalist-dominated network of 25 waterbird species feeding on 9 invertebrate taxa. The Phelps Creek confluence tells a different story: Rails and Willets specialize heavily on oligochaeta worms. This saline-freshwater junction may allow for reduced competition between specialicists and generalists as it creates such a unique niche. Overall, there is high overlap of waterbird species overlap across all four zones, with only the Wilson’s Snipe recorded exclusively at vernal pools.



Takeaways

Food web structure, not just water presence, largely determines how waterbirds end up using restored wetland habitats.
Fine-scale habitat monitoring captures ecologically meaningful variation that broad site-level surveys may miss entirely - this is something restoration managers at other natural reserves can directly work towards developing.


View full poster on eScholarship

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
The North Campus Open Space (NCOS) is an actively restoring wetland adjacent to UC Santa Barbara’s campus. As a Student Research Lead at the Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration, I designed and led a study investigating the spatial and trophic relationships between waterbirds, aquatic invertebrates, and tidal dynamics across four habitat zones at Devereux Slough.



Photograph: Bill Dewey

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
The Question
As NCOS continues to restore tidal connectivity and native habitat, a key management question emerges: how are waterbirds and their invertebrate prey distributed across the wetland, and how does tidal stage influence those patterns? Understanding these linkages can directly inform restoration decisions — where to prioritize habitat work, and what ecological benchmarks to track over time.

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
Methods
I conducted systematic waterbird point-count surveys across four habitat zones while simultaneously collecting aquatic invertebrate samples. I then built bipartite trophic networks linking bird species to invertebrate prey based on diet literature, and analyzed how network structure varied by habitat and tidal stage using GIS-based spatial analysis in ArcGIS and R.

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
Results
This work was presented at the UCSB Undergraduate Research and Creative Activities (URCA) poster colloquium in May 2026.
View full poster on eScholarship

title: Visualizing Waterbird-Aquatic Invertebrates Linkages at NCOS
text:
What We Found
The slough supports the greatest waterbird diversity on site, with a dense, generalist-dominated network connecting 25 waterbird species to nine invertebrate taxa. The Phelps Creek confluence tells a different story — rails and Willets there specialize heavily on Oligochaeta worms, likely because reduced competition with generalists at this unique saline-freshwater junction opens up that niche. Species overlap across all four zones is high, with only the Wilson’s Snipe recorded exclusively in vernal pools.
The big takeaway: food web structure, not just water presence, shapes how waterbirds use restored wetland habitat. Fine-scale habitat monitoring captures ecologically meaningful variation that broad site-level surveys miss entirely — something restoration managers at NCOS can directly act on.
I presented my work at the UCSB Undergraduate Research and Creative Activities (URCA) poster colloquium in May 2026.
View full poster on eScholarship

title: Drought, Agricultural Land-Use Change, and Employment Vulnerability in California’s Central Valley
text:
Overview
California’s Central Valley produces over 25% of the nation’s fruits, nuts, and table foods, and in Fresno and Kern Counties, roughly one in six to seven jobs is tied directly to agriculture. That makes these communities especially vulnerable to water scarcity. Within a single decade, California endured two major drought periods: 2007–2009 and the historically severe 2012–2016 drought. When water becomes scarce, farmers adapt by fallowing land or shifting crop types. Such changes may directly affect the local labor markets in these counties.
This project asks: how are those drought-driven land-use changes in Fresno and Kern Counties associated with regional unemployment patterns from 2010 to 2016?



Project Poster





Explore the StoryMap
The interactive StoryMap below walks through our full analysis, visualizations, and findings in detail.
Open full StoryMap in new tab

title: Drought, Agricultural Land-Use Change, and Employment Vulnerability in California’s Central Valley
text:
California’s Central Valley produces over 25% of the nation’s fruits, nuts, and table foods — and in Fresno and Kern Counties, roughly one in six to seven jobs is tied directly to agriculture. That makes these communities especially exposed when water runs out. Within a single decade, California endured two major drought periods: 2007–2009 and the historically severe 2012–2016 drought. When water becomes scarce, farmers adapt by fallowing land or shifting crop types — changes that ripple directly into local labor markets.
This project asks: how are those drought-driven land-use changes in Fresno and Kern Counties associated with regional unemployment patterns from 2010 to 2016?

title: Drought, Agricultural Land-Use Change, and Employment Vulnerability in California’s Central Valley
text:
Approach
Working with a team of four, I used GIS tools to track these relationships spatially. Agricultural land-use data came from the USDA Cropland Data Layer, which we reclassified into harmonized crop categories and aggregated at the census-tract level. We then joined American Community Survey unemployment rates to the same spatial units and overlaid drought exposure, land-use change, and unemployment to produce hotspot maps and correlation figures — identifying exactly where environmental stress and economic vulnerability converge within each county.

title: Drought, Agricultural Land-Use Change, and Employment Vulnerability in California’s Central Valley
text:
What We Found
Both counties show a sharp spike in agricultural area change and unemployment in 2011 — farmland shifts and labor market stress rose simultaneously. Fresno saw a 66.80% increase in agricultural shape area that year alongside a 41.23% rise in unemployment change. Kern’s numbers were even more dramatic: 113.79% agricultural area change paired with a 31.58% unemployment increase. The two counties also diverge in crop composition — Fresno maintains a more balanced mix of cotton, tomatoes, fallow, and almonds, while Kern shifted heavily toward fallow and idle land as drought intensified.
The core finding: drought doesn’t just affect crops. It restructures entire local economies, and the spatial pattern of that restructuring is visible at the census-tract level when you look closely enough.

title: Drought, Agricultural Land-Use Change, and Employment Vulnerability in California’s Central Valley
text:
Explore the StoryMap
The interactive StoryMap below walks through our full analysis, visualizations, and findings in detail.

title: Drought, Agricultural Land-Use Change, and Employment Vulnerability in California’s Central Valley

title: Snowpack Variability and Water Quality in Lake Tahoe
text:
Overview
Lake Tahoe sits on the California-Nevada border at over 6,000 feet above sea level, and holds some of the clearest water of any lake in the world. That clarity is scenic, though it also supports a multi-billion dollar recreation economy, supports fisheries, and provides drinking a large portion of water for cities in Nevada. Every spring, melting Sierra Nevada snow flows into the lake through rivers like the Upper Truckee, replenishing it for the year ahead.
Our group wanted to know: does the amount of snow in a given year actually change the lake’s water quality? More snowmelt means more freshwater diluting whatever nutrients and sediment are already in the lake, but it also means more runoff carrying new material in. The net effect isn’t obvious, so we set out to measure it remotely, looking at nine years of satellite imagery from 2017 to 2025.



Data
We pulled from three sources:

Sentinel-2 satellite imagery gave us a detailed spectral view of the lake surface every five days, at 10 to 20 meter resolution. It’s been collecting data since 2017, which defined our study window.
SNOTEL stations are automated snowpack sensors maintained by the USDA. We used 11 stations within the Lake Tahoe Basin to get peak snow water equivalent (SWE): think of this as the actual water content of the a given snowpack. Values were extracted for each year.
USGS streamflow records from the Upper Truckee River let us pinpoint when snowmelt was actually entering the lake each year, rather than assuming a fixed calendar date.




Methods
Step 1: Find the melt window
The timing of snowmelt shifts from year to year, so using the same date range each year would be comparing apples to oranges. Instead, we used a Temporal Centroid of Streamflow algorithm: we identified the date each year when 50% of spring discharge had passed through the Upper Truckee. That date became the center of a 30-day window representing peak loading into the lake. In 2017, for example, that center date was May 22, giving us a window of May 12 to June 11.
Step 2: Measure water quality from space
Within each melt window, we calculated two indices from the Sentinel-2 imagery:
NDCI measures chlorophyll-a concentration using the red edge and red bands. More chlorophyll generally means more algal activity.
NDTI measures turbidity using the red and green bands. Higher turbidity means more suspended sediment in the water.
We cloud-masked all imagery before running the analysis to avoid bad pixels skewing results.
Step 3: Compare to snowpack
We randomly sampled 1,000 pixels from each year’s composite image, calculated mean NDCI and NDTI values, and plotted them against that year’s peak snowpack. If bigger snow years produced cleaner or dirtier water, we’d see a trend.



Results
No trend appeared. The R² for NDCI versus snowpack was 0.055, and for NDTI versus snowpack it was just 0.002. In plain terms: knowing how much snow fell in a given year tells you almost nothing about what the lake’s chlorophyll or turbidity looked like during snowmelt season.
Both indices stayed consistently low across all nine years, which is actually good news for the lake. Tahoe is remarkably clean regardless of snowpack conditions. The biggest snow year in our dataset (2023, at 59.2 inches SWE) and the smallest (2022, at 18.2 inches) produced nearly identical water quality images.



Discussion
The result surprised us. We expected bigger snowpack to mean cleaner water through dilution, and it didn’t really affect it.
Part of the explanation may lie in the indices themselves. NDTI was designed for turbid, sediment-heavy water like rivers. In an exceptionally clear alpine lake, algal growth can produce a spectral signature that NDTI misreads as clean water. So the lake could have more biological activity in a high-snowpack year without our turbidity index picking it up. The NDCI metric has similar limitations in ultra-clear water.
Future work would incorporate in-situ water samples to validate the satellite readings, add thermal imagery as an additional variable, and expand the time series as more Sentinel-2 data accumulates… the longer the record, the better chance we have of detecting the real inter-annual patterns.



Project Poster





Project Slides

title: Snowpack Variability and Water Quality in the Lake Tahoe Basin
text:
Overview
Lake Tahoe sits on the California-Nevada border at over 6,000 feet above sea level, and holds some of the clearest water of any lake in the world. That clarity is scenic, though it also supports a multi-billion dollar recreation economy, supports fisheries, and provides drinking a large portion of water for cities in Nevada. Every spring, melting Sierra Nevada snow flows into the lake through rivers like the Upper Truckee, replenishing it for the year ahead.
Our group wanted to know: does the amount of snow in a given year actually change the lake’s water quality? More snowmelt means more freshwater diluting whatever nutrients and sediment are already in the lake, but it also means more runoff carrying new material in. The net effect isn’t obvious, so we set out to measure it remotely, looking at nine years of satellite imagery from 2017 to 2025.

title: Snowpack Variability and Water Quality in the Lake Tahoe Basin
text:
The Question
How does Sierra Nevada snowpack variability influence water clarity and quality conditions in the Lake Tahoe Basin? As climate change reshapes precipitation patterns and shifts the rain-snow transition zone upward in elevation, understanding these snowmelt-driven dynamics becomes increasingly important for long-term lake management.

title: Snowpack Variability and Water Quality in the Lake Tahoe Basin
text:
Approach
Using remote sensing imagery and Google Earth Engine, I analyzed multi-year snowpack patterns across the Sierra Nevada and connected those patterns to downstream water quality indicators in the Tahoe Basin. This project combined time series analysis of satellite-derived snow cover with spatial analysis of basin hydrology to identify relationships between snowpack conditions and lake clarity metrics.

title: Snowpack Variability and Water Quality in the Lake Tahoe Basin
text: Project Poster

title: Snowpack Variability and Water Quality in the Lake Tahoe Basin
text:
Data
We pulled from three sources:

Sentinel-2 satellite imagery gave us a detailed spectral view of the lake surface every five days, at 10 to 20 meter resolution. It’s been collecting data since 2017, which defined our study window.
SNOTEL stations are automated snowpack sensors maintained by the USDA. We used 11 stations within the Lake Tahoe Basin to get peak snow water equivalent (SWE): think of this as the actual water content of the a given snowpack. Values were extracted for each year.
USGS streamflow records from the Upper Truckee River let us pinpoint when snowmelt was actually entering the lake each year, rather than assuming a fixed calendar date.

title: Snowpack Variability and Water Quality in the Lake Tahoe Basin
text:
Methods
Step 1: Find the melt window
The timing of snowmelt shifts from year to year, so using the same date range each year would be comparing apples to oranges. Instead, we used a Temporal Centroid of Streamflow algorithm: we identified the date each year when 50% of spring discharge had passed through the Upper Truckee. That date became the center of a 30-day window representing peak loading into the lake. In 2017, for example, that center date was May 22, giving us a window of May 12 to June 11.
Step 2: Measure water quality from space
Within each melt window, we calculated two indices from the Sentinel-2 imagery:
NDCI measures chlorophyll-a concentration using the red edge and red bands. More chlorophyll generally means more algal activity.
NDTI measures turbidity using the red and green bands. Higher turbidity means more suspended sediment in the water.
We cloud-masked all imagery before running the analysis to avoid bad pixels skewing results.
Step 3: Compare to snowpack
We randomly sampled 1,000 pixels from each year’s composite image, calculated mean NDCI and NDTI values, and plotted them against that year’s peak snowpack. If bigger snow years produced cleaner or dirtier water, we’d see a trend.

title: Snowpack Variability and Water Quality in the Lake Tahoe Basin
text:
Results
No trend appeared. The R² for NDCI versus snowpack was 0.055, and for NDTI versus snowpack it was just 0.002. In plain terms: knowing how much snow fell in a given year tells you almost nothing about what the lake’s chlorophyll or turbidity looked like during snowmelt season.
Both indices stayed consistently low across all nine years, which is actually good news for the lake. Tahoe is remarkably clean regardless of snowpack conditions. The biggest snow year in our dataset (2023, at 59.2 inches SWE) and the smallest (2022, at 18.2 inches) produced nearly identical water quality images.

title: Snowpack Variability and Water Quality in the Lake Tahoe Basin
text:
Discussion & Limitations
Our results did not support the dilution hypothesis. The quantity of snowmelt entering Lake Tahoe does not appear to be a strong driver of chlorophyll or turbidity at the whole-lake scale — at least not as measured by NDCI and NDTI during the peak melt window.
Two important limitations shaped our interpretation. First, NDTI is best suited for highly turbid waters with significant sediment loads (like rivers). In clear alpine water, photosynthetic algal growth can produce high green reflectance with low red reflectance — causing the index to read as “clear” even when algae are present. Second, shallow nearshore areas and docks introduce noise by registering artificially high turbidity and low chlorophyll values.
Future work could incorporate in-situ turbidity and sediment data to validate the satellite-derived indices, expand the independent variables to include thermal imagery, and apply multivariate regression to better isolate what actually drives water quality variation at Tahoe. As Sentinel-2 imagery accumulates past 2025, extending the time series could also reveal longer-term patterns that a 9-year window can’t capture.

title: Snowpack Variability and Water Quality in the Lake Tahoe Basin

Discussion
The result surprised us. We expected bigger snowpack to mean cleaner water through dilution, and it didn’t really affect it.
Part of the explanation may lie in the indices themselves. NDTI was designed for turbid, sediment-heavy water like rivers. In an exceptionally clear alpine lake, algal growth can produce a spectral signature that NDTI misreads as clean water. So the lake could have more biological activity in a high-snowpack year without our turbidity index picking it up. The NDCI metric has similar limitations in ultra-clear water.
Future work would incorporate in-situ water samples to validate the satellite readings, add thermal imagery as an additional variable, and expand the time series as more Sentinel-2 data accumulates… the longer the record, the better chance we have of detecting the real inter-annual patterns.

title: Tracking Post-Fire Vegetation Recovery with a Landsat time Series
text:
Overview
One of the most powerful uses of satellite remote sensing is detecting how land cover changes over long periods time. One single image tells you what an area looks like today, but a time series generates the story of how it got there. In this lab, I used Google Earth Engine (GEE), Landsat 8 imagery, and spectral indices to track vegetation change before and after the 2018 Woolsey Fire in Southern California.
In November 2018, the Woolsey Fire burned through roughly 97,000 acres across the Los Angeles and Ventura Counties, scorching the Santa Monica Mountains found between Thousand Oaks and Malibu. With a remote sensing background, I know a fire that large will leave a clear and highly-measurable signal in satellite data. The core skill applied here is time series analysis: pulling imagery across many dates, calculating a vegetation index for each image, and plotting how that index changes over time… all to track the fire’s impact and the recovery of the landscape.



What is Google Earth Engine?
Google Earth Engine is a free, cloud-based platform created specifically for analyzing satellite imagery. Normally, working with this kind of data means downloading huge files to a hard drive and hoping your computer can handle everything. But GEE fixes this: the user writes code that runs on Google’s servers, right where all of the imagery already lives. As a result, you can analyze decades of global satellite data in mere seconds, and from any computer.
In this lab, I use GEE through Python using the geemap library, which lets you write Python code and visualize results as interactive maps inside a Google Colab notebook (No extra software needed!).



Landsat 8
Landsat is a joint NASA/USGS satellite program that has been continuously imaging Earth’s surface since 1972. This makes it the longest continuous space-based record of Earth’s land surface ever. Each satellite captures multi-spectral imagery, meaning it records light in multiple wavelength bands… beyond just visible colors. It captures near-infrared and shortwave infrared bands, and these are what makes advanced vegetation and water analysis possible.
In this lab I worked with Landsat 8 Surface Reflectance, which captures imagery at a 30-meter resolution with a 16-day revisit time.



Spectral Indices
Rather than analyzing raw band values, remote sensing scientists calculate spectral indices, or ratios of specific band combinations that highlight particular environmental features. The index used in this lab was:
NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) Calculated as (NIR - Red) / (NIR + Red). Values range from -1 to 1. Healthy green vegetation reflects strongly in near-infrared and absorbs red light, producing high positive values. Bare soil, water, and developed areas produce values near zero or negative.



Code Walkthrough
Here is the core workflow from the lab. Each step is annotated to explain what it does.

Step 1: Setup and Processing
Before calculating anything, raw Landsat imagery needs to be cleaned. Clouds, cloud shadows, and saturated pixels all introduce “noise” that would would impede analysis. I wrote a preprocessing function that applies quality assurance masks and scaling factors to every image in the collection automatically. Finally, I filtered the data to 2018 and 2019 to capture pre-fire and post-fire conditions.
import ee

# Authenticate your Google account to access GEE
ee.Authenticate()
# Initialize with your project
ee.Initialize(project = "geog115c-spring2026")

# Mount Google Drive for saving figures
from pathlib import Path
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

out_dir = Path('/content/drive/MyDrive/GEOG 115C')
out_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

# Load Landsat 8 surface reflectance imagery for all of 2018 and 2019
!pip install geemap
import geemap

dataset = ee.ImageCollection('LANDSAT/LC08/C02/T1_L2').filterDate(
    '2018-01-01', '2019-12-31'
)

# Preprocessing function: masks bad pixels and applies scaling factors
def preprocess_landsat8(image):
    # QA masks
    qa_mask = image.select('QA_PIXEL').bitwiseAnd(int('11111', 2)).eq(0)
    # Each 1 in the above line corresponds to each bitmask value (i.e., Fill,
    # Dilated Cloud, Cirrius (high confidence), Cloud, and Cloud Shadow). The 2
    # tells us to interpret this as a base-2 (binary) number. The eq(0) indicates
    # we only want to include the highest value data (i.e., where those bitmask
    # values are equal to 0; eq(1) would indicate the poor quality data. In doing this,
    # eq(0) ensures we only keep pixels where NONE of these flags are noted).
    saturation_mask = image.select('QA_RADSAT').eq(0)

    # Apply scaling factors to raw DN
    optical = image.select('SR_B.*').multiply(0.0000275).add(-0.2)
    thermal = image.select('ST_B.*').multiply(0.00341802).add(149.0)

    return (
        image
        .addBands(optical, None, True)
        .addBands(thermal, None, True)
        .updateMask(qa_mask)
        .updateMask(saturation_mask)
    )

visualization = {
    'bands': ['SR_B4', 'SR_B3', 'SR_B2'],
    'min': 0.0,
    'max': 0.3,
}

# Apply preprocessing to every image in the collection
image = dataset.map(preprocess_landsat8)

# Visualize as a true color composite to confirm data loaded correctly
m = geemap.Map()
m.set_center(-114.2579, 38.9275, 8)
m.add_layer(image, visualization, 'True Color')
m



Preprocessed Landsat 8 true color imagery loaded in GEE





Step 2: Define the study area
Rather than spending an hour to delineate a study area by hand, I loaded a publicly available Woolsey Fire boundary as a GeoJSON file from CAL FIRE. A GeoJSON is a standard file format that geographers use to store vectors (points, lines, polygons) as plain text that both humans and code can read.
I used the geopandas library to read the file and geemap library to convert it into a GEE geometry object. From there, I was able to clip all of the analysis to only the burned area.
import geopandas as gpd

# Path to the Woolsey Fire GeoJSON stored in Google Drive
geojson_path = '/content/drive/My Drive/Colab Notebooks/Geog115c_2026/geojsons/'
geojson = geojson_path + 'Woolsey_Fire.geojson'

# Read the GeoJSON into a spatial dataframe
WoolseyFire_Perimeter = gpd.read_file(geojson)

# Convert to a GEE feature so we can use it
WoolseyFire_Perimeter_gee = geemap.geopandas_to_ee(WoolseyFire_Perimeter)

# Extract just the perimeter for clipping and analysis
woolsey_geom = WoolseyFire_Perimeter_gee.geometry()

# Visualize the fire perimeter on a map
map = geemap.Map()
map.addLayer(woolsey_geom, {}, 'Woolsey Fire Perimeter')
map



Woolsey Fire perimeter loaded into GEE





Step 3: Calculating NDVI and building time series
Now that the study area is defined, I moved on to calculate NDVI for every image in the collection and tracking how it changed over time within the burned area. The function compute_timeseries does a lot of the work here. For every image, it calculates the mean NDVI across all pixels within the fire perimeter, and stores that value along with the image’s date… a time series! The result is a table of NDVI values paired with timestamps, called a feature collection. The visual here is a median composite image, confirming our NDVI function worked.
# Defining an NDVI calculation function
def calc_ndvi(image):
    # Calculates NDVI from Landsat 8 SR bands and returns image with NDVI as
    # another band
    # NIR = SR_B5 and Red = SR_B4
    ndvi = image.normalizedDifference(['SR_B5', 'SR_B4']).rename("NDVI")
    return image.addBands(ndvi)

# Testing function on median image composite
median_image = image.median()
median_ndvi = calc_ndvi(median_image)

print("Median Image and Bands:", median_ndvi.bandNames().getInfo())

# Visualizing the median NDVI image
ndvi_vis = {
    "bands": ["NDVI"],
    "min": -0.2,
    "max": 0.8,
    "palette": ["red", "yellow", "green"]
}

m2 = geemap.Map()
m2.set_center(-118, 34, 9)
m2.add_layer(median_ndvi, ndvi_vis, "Median NDVI")
m2



NDVI median composite across Southern California. Green is healthy vegetation, red and orange are bare or stressed areas.


# Apply NDVI calculation to the entire image collection
ndvi_collection = image.map(calc_ndvi)

# compute_timeseries maps over each image independently, then uses reduceRegion
# to calculate mean NDVI within the ROI for that specific image, saving both
# the NDVI and image time values in an ee.Feature. The ee.FeatureCollection
# can then be used for a timeseries because it contains the date of each image.
def compute_timeseries(ImageCollection, region):
    def per_image(img):
        mean_dict = img.reduceRegion(
            reducer=ee.Reducer.mean(),
            geometry=region,
            scale=30,
            bestEffort=True
        )
        return ee.Feature(
            None,
            {
                'NDVI': mean_dict.get('NDVI'),
                'time': img.date().millis()
            }
        )
    return ee.FeatureCollection(
        ImageCollection.filterBounds(region).map(per_image)
    )

# Calling compute_timeseries for the Woolsey Fire perimeter
woolsey_timeseries = compute_timeseries(ndvi_collection, woolsey_geom)

# Converting to a useable dataframe
woolsey_df = geemap.ee_to_df(woolsey_timeseries)[["NDVI", "time"]]

print(woolsey_df.head())
print(f"\nRows: {len(woolsey_df)}")



Step 4: Analysis and Visualization
With the data in a pandas DataFrame, it was time to plot the time series.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Converting time column to datetime
# "time" was stored in milliseconds, therefore unit = "ms"
woolsey_df["time"] = pd.to_datetime(woolsey_df["time"], unit = "ms")

# Drop rows missing NDVI
woolsey_df = woolsey_df.dropna(subset = ["NDVI"])

# Sort values by time
woolsey_df = woolsey_df.sort_values("time").reset_index(drop = True)

# Plotting NDVI v. time
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))

ax.scatter(
    woolsey_df['time'],
    woolsey_df['NDVI'],
    s=15,
    color='green',
    label='Fire Scar as NDVI'
)

ax.set_xlabel('Date', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('NDVI', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('NDVI over Time in the Woolsey Fire Perimeter (2018-2019)', fontsize=13)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.matplotlib.dates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
plt.xticks(rotation=45)

plt.show()



Raw NDVI time series within the Woolsey Fire perimeter


As you can see, the fire shows up clearly. NDVI inside the perimeter drops sharply in late 2018, then slowly starts climbing back through 2019 as vegetation starts to recover.
To give that drop some more context, I added a second time series from a nearby unburned area in the Santa Monica Mountains as a baseline to compare. If the fire caused a real change in vegetation, and not just normal seasonal variation, the two lines should dramatically diverge after the November 2018 date.
# Defining a baseline geometry (unburned area in the Santa Monica Mtns)
baseline_geom = ee.Geometry.BBox(-118.5, 34.05, -118.35, 34.15)

# Calculate the NDVI timeseries for the baseline region
baseline_timeseries = compute_timeseries(ndvi_collection, baseline_geom)

# Convert to dataframe and clean
baseline_df = geemap.ee_to_df(baseline_timeseries)[["NDVI", "time"]]

baseline_df["time"] = pd.to_datetime(baseline_df["time"], unit="ms")
baseline_df = baseline_df.dropna(subset=["NDVI"])
baseline_df = baseline_df.sort_values("time").reset_index(drop=True)

# Plotting both timeseries together
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))

ax.scatter(
    woolsey_df["time"],
    woolsey_df["NDVI"],
    s = 25,
    color = "red",
    label="Woolsey Fire Scar"
)
ax.scatter(
    baseline_df["time"],
    baseline_df["NDVI"],
    s = 18,
    color = "blue",
    label = 'Unburned baseline'
)

ax.set_xlabel("Date", fontsize = 12)
ax.set_ylabel("NDVI", fontsize = 12)
ax.set_title("Woolsey Fire Scar vs. Unburned Baseline NDVI Timeseries (2018-2019)",
             fontsize = 13)
ax.legend(fontsize = 11)
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.matplotlib.dates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
plt.xticks(rotation=45)

plt.show()



Fire scar vs. unburned baseline NDVI. The divergence after November 2018 is clear.


Before the Woolsey fire, both areas track each other pretty closely through the year. After the fire, the burned area drops hard while the unburned baseline just keeps doing its thing. There are significant vegetation impacts from the fire.



Step 5: LOWESS smoothing
Even with clean data, individual satellite images can still be thrown off by leftover haze, thin clouds, or other atmospheric noise. These show up as random spikes or dips in the scatter plot. To smooth them out, and without losing the raw data, I applied a technique called LOWESS (Locally Weighted Scatterplot Smoothing). It is essentially just drawing a best-fit line through the data, but not just one straight line across everything, the algorithm fits a bunch of smaller, local curves that follow the shape of the data more naturally. The frac parameter controls how tight those local fits are. Here, I used 0.15, meaning each smoothed point considers 15% of the surrounding data when fitting its curve.
# Applying temporal smoothing

# Import LOWESS smoothing function
from statsmodels.nonparametric.smoothers_lowess import lowess

def lowess_smooth(df, var, frac=0.15):
    smoothed = lowess(
        endog=df[var],
        exog=df['time'].astype('int64'),
        frac=frac,
        return_sorted=False
    )
    return smoothed

# Applying the smoothing to create new columns in each dataframe
# (creating a smoothed NDVI timeseries)
woolsey_df["NDVI_smooth"] = lowess_smooth(woolsey_df, "NDVI")
baseline_df["NDVI_smooth"] = lowess_smooth(baseline_df, "NDVI")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize = (13, 6))

# Raw data = points
ax.scatter(
    woolsey_df["time"],  woolsey_df["NDVI"],
    s = 19,
    color = "red",
    label = "Fire Scar (raw)"
)
ax.scatter(
    baseline_df["time"],
    baseline_df["NDVI"],
    s = 19,
    color = "blue",
    label = "Baseline (raw)"
)

# Smoothed = lines
ax.plot(
    woolsey_df['time'],
    woolsey_df['NDVI_smooth'],
    color = "red",
    linewidth = 2.5,
    label = "Fire Scar (LOWESS)"
)
ax.plot(
    baseline_df["time"],
    baseline_df["NDVI_smooth"],
    color = "blue",
    linewidth = 2.5,
    label = "Baseline (LOWESS)"
)

ax.set_xlabel("Date", fontsize = 12)
ax.set_ylabel("NDVI", fontsize = 12)
ax.set_title(
    "NDVI Timeseries with LOWESS Smoothing:\nWoolsey Fire Scar vs. Unburned Baseline (2018-2019)",
    fontsize = 14
)

# Legend and labels
ax.legend(fontsize = 12)
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.matplotlib.dates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
plt.xticks(rotation = 45)

plt.show()



NDVI time series with LOWESS smoothing applied. Fire scar in red, unburned baseline in blue.


As you can see, the smoothed curves make the story easy to read. The fire scar NDVI abruptly crashes in late 2018, starts to recover through the 2019 spring, and gradually returns to baseline patterns from there. That unburned baseline shows expected seasonal changes throughout the whole period. This visual gave me confidence in saying that the vegetation loss inside the fire perimeter was real and caused by the fire itself, not just a normal part of the season.

title: Land Cover Change Detection Using Landsat Time Series in Google Earth Engine
text:
One of the most powerful applications of satellite remote sensing is detecting how land cover changes over time. A single image tells you what somewhere looks like today. A time series tells you the story of how it got there. In this lab, I used Google Earth Engine (GEE) — a cloud-based platform that gives you access to decades of satellite imagery without downloading a single file — to analyze land cover change using Landsat imagery and spectral indices.
The core skill here is time series analysis: pulling imagery across many years, calculating an index for each image, and plotting how that index changes over time. This kind of analysis is used in real-world hydrology and ecology work to track vegetation stress, drought impacts, urban expansion, and water body changes.

title: Land Cover Change Detection Using Landsat Time Series in Google Earth Engine
text:
What is Google Earth Engine?
Google Earth Engine is a cloud computing platform built specifically for geospatial analysis. Instead of downloading satellite images to your computer, you write code that runs on Google’s servers — which means you can analyze decades of global imagery in seconds. In this course we used GEE through Python (via the geemap library), which lets you write familiar Python code and visualize results in an interactive map directly in a Jupyter notebook.

title: Land Cover Change Detection Using Landsat Time Series in Google Earth Engine
text:
The Data: Landsat
Landsat is a joint NASA/USGS satellite program that has been continuously imaging Earth’s surface since 1972 — the longest continuous space-based record of Earth’s land surface that exists. Each satellite captures multispectral imagery, meaning it records light in multiple wavelength bands beyond just visible color, including near-infrared and shortwave infrared. These extra bands are what make vegetation and water analysis possible.
In this lab I worked with Landsat 8, which captures imagery at 30 meter resolution with a 16-day revisit time.

title: Land Cover Change Detection Using Landsat Time Series in Google Earth Engine
text:
Spectral Indices
Rather than analyzing raw band values, remote sensing scientists calculate spectral indices — ratios of band combinations that highlight specific features. Two indices were central to this lab:
NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) Calculated as (NIR - Red) / (NIR + Red). Values range from -1 to 1. Healthy green vegetation reflects strongly in near-infrared and absorbs red light, producing high positive values. Bare soil, water, and developed areas produce values near zero or negative.
NDWI (Normalized Difference Water Index) Calculated as (Green - NIR) / (Green + NIR). Highlights water bodies and vegetation water content. Positive values indicate open water or moisture-stressed vegetation.

title: Land Cover Change Detection Using Landsat Time Series in Google Earth Engine
text:
Code Walkthrough
Here is the core workflow from the lab. Each step is annotated to explain what it does and why.
Step 1: Authenticate and initialize Google Earth Engine
import ee
import geemap

# Authenticate your Google account to access GEE
ee.Authenticate()

# Initialize the Earth Engine API
ee.Initialize()
Step 2: Define the study area
# Define a point of interest - GEE will center the analysis here
# Coordinates are longitude, latitude
point = ee.Geometry.Point([-119.5, 37.5])

# Buffer the point to create a region of interest (50km radius)
roi = point.buffer(50000)
Step 3: Load and filter the Landsat 8 image collection
# Load Landsat 8 Surface Reflectance collection
# Surface Reflectance means atmospheric correction has already been applied
landsat = ee.ImageCollection('LANDSAT/LC08/C02/T1_L2') \
    .filterBounds(roi) \           # Only images that overlap our region
    .filterDate('2014-01-01', '2023-12-31') \  # Date range: 2014-2023
    .filter(ee.Filter.lt('CLOUD_COVER', 20))   # Less than 20% cloud cover
Step 4: Calculate NDVI for each image
# Define a function to calculate NDVI and add it as a new band
def calculate_ndvi(image):
    # Select NIR (Band 5) and Red (Band 4) from Landsat 8
    nir = image.select('SR_B5')
    red = image.select('SR_B4')
    
    # Calculate NDVI using the normalizedDifference method
    ndvi = image.normalizedDifference(['SR_B5', 'SR_B4']).rename('NDVI')
    
    # Add NDVI as a new band to the image and return
    return image.addBands(ndvi)

# Apply the function to every image in the collection using .map()
landsat_ndvi = landsat.map(calculate_ndvi)
Step 5: Extract time series and plot
# Create a chart of mean NDVI over time within the region of interest
import geemap.chart as chart

# Reduce each image to a single mean NDVI value for the ROI
timeseries = landsat_ndvi.select('NDVI').map(
    lambda img: img.reduceRegion(
        reducer=ee.Reducer.mean(),
        geometry=roi,
        scale=30
    )
)

# Plot the time series
chart.image_series(
    image_collection=landsat_ndvi,
    region=roi,
    reducer=ee.Reducer.mean(),
    scale=30,
    x_property='system:time_start',
    y_property='NDVI'
)
Step 6: Visualize on an interactive map
# Create an interactive map centered on our region
Map = geemap.Map()
Map.centerObject(roi, zoom=8)

# Define visualization parameters for NDVI
# Green palette: low NDVI = white/yellow, high NDVI = dark green
ndvi_vis = {
    'min': -0.2,
    'max': 0.8,
    'palette': ['white', 'yellow', 'green', 'darkgreen']
}

# Add a single year's NDVI composite to the map
ndvi_2023 = landsat_ndvi \
    .filterDate('2023-06-01', '2023-09-01') \
    .median() \
    .select('NDVI')

Map.addLayer(ndvi_2023, ndvi_vis, 'NDVI 2023')
Map.addLayerControl()
Map

title: Land Cover Change Detection Using Landsat Time Series in Google Earth Engine
text:
What the Results Show
The time series plot reveals clear seasonal patterns in NDVI — values peak each summer as vegetation reaches full greenness, then drop through fall and winter. Across the multi-year record, you can also start to see inter-annual variation: drought years show suppressed summer peaks, while wet years show earlier and stronger green-up. This kind of signal is exactly what hydrologists and ecologists use to track ecosystem response to climate variability.

title: Land Cover Change Detection Using Landsat Time Series in Google Earth Engine
text:
Why This Matters
Time series analysis in GEE is one of the most transferable skills in modern environmental science. The same workflow used here to track vegetation greenness can be redirected to monitor reservoir water levels, track post-fire recovery, detect illegal deforestation, or flag anomalies in agricultural fields. The cloud-based architecture means these analyses scale globally without expensive hardware — which is why GEE has become a standard tool in hydrology, ecology, and land management.
